title: David Thomson spent 60 years celebrating cinema. Now he thinks movies may have ruined us
description: David Thomson spent 60 years celebrating cinema. Now he thinks movies may have ruined us
hook: A lifelong film critic finally admits what he's been avoiding: cinema didn't elevate us—it made us passive, distracted, and perpetually unsatisfied.

title: The Bear Bids a Profoundly Warm Farewell
description: The series finale is a moving hour of much-needed stress relief.
hook: The Bear ends not with chaos but tenderness. This is what happens when a show about masculine perfectionism finally lets its characters breathe.

title: O My Body
description: In 1976's imagined version of 2274, do Black people exist? According to Logan’s Run, you’d think the answer is no.
hook: Logan's Run imagined the future in 1976 and erased Black people entirely. What that absence reveals about sci-fi's racial imagination.

title: Tunnel 13
description: "A botched train robbery in Oregon’s Siskiyou Mountains left four men dead, led to an international manhunt, and helped usher forensic science into the modern age."
hook: A bungled train robbery in 1923 became the first major test of forensic science—a story about how crime scenes became readable.

title: The Popularity Contests of “Love Island”
description: Most romantic reality TV would have us believe that dating is about getting married, or simply being chosen. One show knows better.
hook: Love Island knows something other reality TV refuses to admit: people want to be wanted more than they want to win.

title: A Short Defense of Sports Clichés
description: On "the perceived chasm between a great athlete’s genius and their apparent inability to talk about it after the fact."
hook: Why the clichés athletes repeat after games aren't failures of language—they're the sound of a mind protecting itself from exposure.

title: Claire Fuller: ‘Dylan Thomas showed me that writing could make me feel everything’
description:
The novelist on being inspired by Shirley Jackson, discovering the brilliance of Denis Johnson, and finding comfort in Elizabeth Strout

My earliest reading memory
When I was five and starting school, I would catch a coach from the Oxfordshire village where I lived. Twice a day I read the little metal plaque screwed to the upholstery, which gave the warning “Mind your head when leaving your seat”.

My favourite book growing up
In
hook: Claire Fuller on discovering that Dylan Thomas and Denis Johnson wrote like they had something to lose. How reading shaped her own reckless sentences.
